concerns.text: The spec assumes using 'existing public IAT datasets' to retroactively assign complexity levels. This violates the Constitution Principle VI (Stimulus Manipulation Integrity) which explicitly prohibits retroactive assignment. Scientifically, this design cannot test the causal hypothesis (complexity -> bias) because the complexity was not manipulated; it only tests an associational link between pre-existing image properties and pre-existing scores, which is confounded by the original experimental design.

FR-003 mandates a 'repeated-measures ANOVA' on D-scores across complexity levels. If the design relies on existing data (as per Assumption), participants likely did not see both low and high complexity backgrounds in a controlled counterbalanced manner. Applying repeated-measures ANOVA to data where the 'conditions' are retroactively assigned based on image properties (which may not have been presented to the same participant in both forms) is a category error and statistically invalid for the claimed causal inference.

The assumption states the relationship is 'associational, not causal' due to existing data. However, the User Story 3 and FR-003 aim to 'determine if visual complexity significantly reduces implicit bias' (causal language). The spec simultaneously claims a causal research question while designing an observational study. This is a fundamental scientific contradiction: you cannot validate a causal mechanism (cognitive load diverting attention) with a design that explicitly admits it cannot support causal inference.

The assumption allows 'automatically generating synthetic background stimuli' if original images are missing. This introduces a massive confound: synthetic images have different semantic content and visual properties than real-world stimuli used in IATs. Comparing IAT scores derived from real stimuli (with unknown complexity) against synthetic stimuli (with known complexity) invalidates the internal validity of the experiment, as the effect could be due to semantic differences or the 'synthetic' nature of the image, not just complexity.
